summary: In this PyCon Colombia 2020 keynote, Fernando Pérez traces his journey from studying physics at Universidad de Antioquia in Medellín to creating IPython and co-founding Jupyter, emphasizing how open source communities transformed scientific computing. He describes how a personal crisis during his PhD led him to Python and IPython, which grew from a 260-line script into a global ecosystem. Pérez argues that successful open source projects must be understood as layered systems encompassing software, content, open standards, governance, and community — not just code. He highlights Jupyter's impact on Nobel Prize-winning research, climate science, and education at scale, including Berkeley's data science curriculum reaching half the student body. The talk serves as both an origin story and an invitation for Latin American developers to join and shape these communities.
key_points: IPython was born during a personal crisis in grad school, motivated by the need for accessible scientific tools, the epistemological absurdity of doing science with proprietary software, and a love for Python's elegance
Successful open source projects must be understood in layers beyond code: content and services, open standards and protocols, governance structures, and community — each amplifying impact

The scientific Python community and the broader Python community have a symbiotic relationship, each contributing strengths the other lacks — engineering practices and community structure from one side, research tools and institutional funding experience from the other
Jupyter's open protocol design enabled an ecosystem of 100+ language kernels, proving that abstracting ideas into open standards multiplies impact far beyond any single implementation

The same tools used for cutting-edge research (gravitational waves, black hole imaging, climate data) serve directly as educational tools, eliminating the gap between teaching with toys and working with real instruments
money_quotes: The idea of doing scientific research with tools that legally prevent you from understanding how they work — someone tells you, you can understand how nature works, but you cannot understand how your tool works. That seems completely absurd to me.

If I look at what's there, the code is a mess. But for some reason I said, this works, I like it, this works, I'm using it, let's put it out there, let's give it to the public because for the user it works.

The credit for Jupyter is not Jupyter's. The credit for Jupyter is Pandas. If it weren't for Wes, much of this wouldn't have had the impact we've had. If it weren't for John Hunter, who spent years creating Matplotlib.

We're not teaching students with toys. We're not teaching with a toy thing that they'll have to abandon when they go out into the real world — we're teaching them literally with the same tools that researchers wrote for their own problems.

When we were creating all of this, they told us explicitly: stop doing this, this is a waste of time, this is worthless, this isn't real work. And knowing that one of the most important results in physics in recent years was partly done with this — that is incredibly satisfying.
The project is the people, the community. It's not two or three people, it's not the code, but a team of people.
Taking the time to go from free software to a series of documented, abstracted ideas defined as an open standard allows an entire ecosystem to grow.
key_insight: The lasting impact of open source scientific software comes not from the code itself but from layering open standards, governance, and inclusive community on top of it — transforming tools into ecosystems that serve both cutting-edge research and universal education.
